title: Hyperfine-resolved laser excitation and detection of nuclear isomer in trapped $^{229}$Th$^{3+}$ ions
authors: Wu Wang, Ke Zhang, Ke-Mi Xu, Shan-Gui Zhou
abstract: We present a comprehensive theoretical investigation of hyperfine-resolved excitation and detection of the low-energy isomeric state of $^{229}$Th in trapped $^{229}\mathrm{Th}^{3+}$ ions. Using a quantum master equation approach, we analyze the dependence of the isomeric population on laser linewidth, detuning, and irradiation time, showing that their proper matching is essential for efficient excitation. We further propose two nuclear-state detection schemes based on three hyperfine-resolved electronic fluorescence channels at 690, 984, and 1088 nm. Our analysis shows that the 690-nm and 984-nm scheme yields detectable photon rates on the order of $10^4~\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ per ion for each wavelength, whereas the 1088-nm scheme achieves a higher rate on the order of $10^5~\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ per ion. By quantifying the trade-off between irradiation time and scan-step size, we show that the nuclear transition can be located within one month for a 100-MHz uncertainty using currently available vacuum-ultraviolet laser technology. These results provide practical guidance for trapped-ion $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ spectroscopy and the development of nuclear clocks.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework for the excitation and detection of the Th-229 nuclear isomer in trapped ions. It proposes specific fluorescence-based detection schemes and demonstrates that the nuclear transition can be identified within a month using existing laser technology. These findings are crucial for the practical implementation of next-generation nuclear clocks.
main_problem: Optimizing the hyperfine-resolved laser excitation and detection of the low-energy nuclear isomeric state of Thorium-229 in trapped ions.
main_result: The study identifies two effective detection schemes using fluorescence channels at 690, 984, and 1088 nm and demonstrates that the nuclear transition can be located within one month using current VUV technology.
method: A quantum master equation approach is used to analyze the dependence of isomeric population on laser parameters and to quantify photon detection rates.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of Th-229+ ions trapped in an ion trap, focusing on the hyperfine-resolved electronic and nuclear transitions.
key_observables: Isomeric population, photon detection rates (per ion), and laser linewidth/detuning/irradiation time dependencies.
important_parameters: Laser linewidth, detuning, irradiation time, and specific fluorescence wavelengths (690, 984, and 1088 nm).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the use of currently available vacuum-ultraviolet (VUV) laser technology and specific fluorescence channels.
paper_structure: The paper presents a theoretical investigation of excitation dynamics, proposes two detection schemes based on fluorescence, and evaluates the practical feasibility of locating the nuclear transition.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and AMO physics as it provides a roadmap for the development of nuclear clocks and the control of nuclear states using atomic-scale laser techniques.
